Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 29, working and managing life, so I couldn't stick to a perfect schedule. I'd grab time when I could—sometimes late at night, sometimes early morning before everything else started. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd print materials and work through problems physically rather than just reading on screen. The 24 hours I put in weekly were scattered across whenever I could find pockets of time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have intense study sessions when something was due. Being an older student with limited resources means I have to juggle a lot, so regular timing just wasn't realistic for me. I tried to be consistent but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered something was coming up or when I felt guilty about not checking in for a while. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would prompt me to go see what was happening. Other times I'd log in just to watch videos because that was easier than reading when I was tired. The sadness I carry makes it hard to motivate myself sometimes, so logging in often felt like a small victory.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more automatic, usually when I was really invested in a particular topic. I'd log in during my lunch break or first thing in the morning. But those habits never really stuck for long—something would happen, my mood would dip, and I'd fall out of the routine. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but changing them is still hard.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful—I watched a lot of them, over 140 minutes worth. As a kinesthetic learner, I appreciated when videos showed processes or demonstrations I could follow along with. The discussion posts were also useful because interacting with peers made the material feel more real and less isolating. When you're dealing with sadness, that connection matters more than people realize.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the smaller assignments, honestly. My assignment completion was really low because I'd prioritize what seemed most important for exams. Some resources felt too text-heavy and I'd zone out trying to get through them. When you're stressed and sad, you have to pick your battles, and sometimes that meant letting certain things slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it involved actually doing something rather than passive reading. I also valued things that didn't take forever—quick, focused activities that I could complete and feel accomplished about. With my mood being what it is, those small wins really matter for keeping me going.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones had urgency attached, so they got priority even when I didn't feel like doing them. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was already struggling to keep up with the essentials. It's frustrating because sometimes the optional content looked genuinely useful, but I just couldn't justify the time.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and print them out so I could highlight and write notes in the margins—that's how I actually learn. I also used external YouTube videos and other resources that explained concepts differently. My LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes spread across my kitchen table.
Q10: Many nights I'd be studying from printed materials or handwritten notes while the LMS showed nothing. I'd work through practice problems on paper, re-watch downloaded videos offline, or study with a friend who'd share their notes. The times I was most deeply engaged with the material often happened completely disconnected from the platform. My exam score reflects learning that happened largely outside the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable and data costs money, so downloading made sense. I'd watch a lecture video once online, then review my notes from it multiple times without ever logging back in. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the repeated studying that followed.
Q12: There were definitely darker periods when engaging felt almost impossible. When my sadness was at its worst, even logging in felt overwhelming. Financial stress would also pull my attention away—hard to focus on coursework when you're worried about basic expenses. The CBT sessions helped me push through some of those times, but they didn't make the underlying struggles disappear.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue from working and studying made me less likely to engage with anything that required deep concentration. Confusion would make me avoid certain topics entirely rather than face feeling stupid. When competing responsibilities piled up, the LMS was often the first thing I'd let slide because it felt less immediately urgent than other life demands.
Q14: Plenty of times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but I'd be thinking about bills or personal problems. Sometimes I'd log in and click around without really absorbing anything—going through the motions because I felt like I should be doing something. Those sessions probably looked productive in the data but they weren't meaningful learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. The sadness made starting things feel impossible some days—there's this weight that makes everything harder. I knew they were important but I'd convince myself I'd do them tomorrow, and tomorrow would come with its own problems. It's a cycle I'm aware of but haven't fully broken.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and avoid. When a deadline approached, I'd have these intense bursts of activity trying to catch up. The problem is that approach meant I was always stressed and never felt on top of things. My decent exam scores came from cramming rather than steady preparation.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. With my mood already being low, negative feedback hit harder than it probably should have. I had to work on this in CBT—not taking academic criticism so personally.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my chaotic schedule, bad because without strict structure I'd procrastinate. Having four online courses meant I was constantly juggling and sometimes this particular course got less attention. The structure didn't force engagement, so my natural tendency to drift took over.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn passively when I was tired. But the heavy reading requirements made things harder—I struggle to stay focused on long texts, especially when I'm sad. The discussion forums were actually helpful because they broke up the isolation. What made it harder was when assignments required multiple steps or resources I didn't have easy access to.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity because I was genuinely trying to learn and engaging with materials, but low submission because completing and turning things in is a different skill than learning. Sometimes you're researching, watching, reading, preparing—but the final step of submitting feels like too much. Depression makes that last push incredibly difficult even when you've done most of the work.
Q21: That also resonates with my experience. My exam score was 88, which is strong, but my LMS activity doesn't fully explain that. I was learning effectively through offline methods—printing, handwriting notes, discussing with others outside the platform. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to absorb material. The LMS captures presence, not necessarily learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people log in, what they click on, how long they stay. They can identify students who disappear entirely. But they miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is genuinely absorbing material or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture sadness or resilience.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and making an effort to understand it, not just complete it. It's showing up mentally even when it's hard. For me, engagement looks like printing materials, writing notes, discussing ideas with peers, and actually thinking about how concepts connect. It's not just logging in—it's being present and invested in your own learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my emotional journey—the days I pushed through sadness to study, the CBT sessions that helped me keep going, the resilience it took to earn a distinction while feeling this way. It wouldn't show the offline hours, the printed notes covered in highlighter, the conversations with peers that helped concepts click. It wouldn't show that behind my scattered engagement was someone really trying despite everything working against her.
